description: Fix commit graph refresh after push/fetch/remote operations and make the graph performant with large repos (many branches, thousands of commits). Plus GitHub OAuth Device Flow login and GitLab multi-account.

userStories.title: Force Graph Refresh After Push, Fetch, Pull
userStories.description: As a user, I want the commit graph to update remote ref tags after push/fetch/pull without having to close and reopen the repo.
acceptanceCriteria: After a successful push (Toolbar push button), the commit graph reloads and remote ref labels (origin/main etc.) update
After a successful fetch, the graph reloads to show new remote commits and updated remote refs
After a successful pull, the graph reloads
The fix: git push/fetch/pull don't change local working tree files so onRepoChanged watcher doesn't fire. After these operations, dispatch a manual refresh event
Add a custom event 'graph:force-refresh' that CommitGraph listens for and triggers loadCommits()
Toolbar.tsx (or wherever push/fetch/pull are triggered) dispatches 'graph:force-refresh' after successful operations
The loadCommits() skip-if-unchanged check (first+last hash comparison) must also compare refs — after push, commits are the same but refs change
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: CommitGraph.tsx loadCommits() on line 1047-1053 skips updates if first+last hashes match. After push, hashes ARE the same but refs on commits change (origin/main moves). Need to also compare ref strings or force a full reload after push/fetch/pull. The graph:force-refresh event should bypass the skip-if-unchanged check.

userStories.title: Limit Initial Commit Load with Pagination
userStories.description: As a user, I want the commit graph to load quickly even on repos with thousands of commits and many branches.
acceptanceCriteria: By default, load only the most recent 500 commits (git log --all -n 500)
Show a 'Load more commits' button or automatic lazy loading at the bottom of the graph
Clicking 'Load more' fetches the next 500 commits (using --skip or date-based pagination)
The total commit count is shown in the graph header: 'Showing 500 of 12,345 commits'
The lane assignment algorithm handles incremental data without recalculating everything
The canvas fallback threshold (50,000) still applies for rendering
Filters (author, date, grep) still work — they're passed to git log alongside the limit
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Currently git log is called with { all: true } and no maxCount. Add maxCount: 500 to the options. For 'load more', track the skip offset and append to existing commits. The git-service.ts log method already supports maxCount option. The graph header currently shows 'N COMMITS' — update to show 'Showing N of total'.

userStories.title: Collapse Inactive Branches in Graph
userStories.description: As a user, I want the graph to be readable even with many branches by collapsing inactive ones.
acceptanceCriteria: Branches with no commits in the visible range are not shown as lanes (they don't take up horizontal space)
The lane assignment algorithm only assigns lanes to branches that have visible commits
Merged branches that have no unmerged commits collapse into the target branch lane
The maximum number of visible lanes is capped (e.g., 15-20) — additional branches are shown as a single 'N more branches' indicator
Users can expand collapsed branches by clicking the indicator
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: The lane assignment in laneAssignment.ts assigns a new lane for each branch divergence. With many branches, this creates dozens of lanes making the graph very wide and unreadable. GitKraken solves this by only showing active lanes and collapsing merged branches. The key change is in assignLanes() — track which lanes have visible commits and skip/merge inactive ones.

userStories.title: Configurable Commit History Depth
userStories.description: As a user, I want to configure how many commits to load in the graph.
acceptanceCriteria: Settings > Git section has a 'Commit history depth' option: 500, 1000, 2500, 5000, All
The selected depth is used as maxCount in the git log call
Default is 500
The setting persists via useSettings
Changing the setting triggers a graph reload
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Add commitHistoryDepth to the settings interface in useSettings.ts. Pass it through to CommitGraph via RepoView. CommitGraph uses it as the maxCount for git log.

userStories.title: Refresh Status Bar and Sidebar After Remote Operations
userStories.description: As a user, I want the status bar ahead/behind counts and sidebar branch list to update after push/fetch/pull.
acceptanceCriteria: After push: status bar ahead/behind updates (ahead count should decrease to 0)
After fetch: status bar shows new incoming count, sidebar shows new remote branches if any
After pull: status bar updates, sidebar branch list refreshes
Use the same 'graph:force-refresh' event from US-GR-001 — other components also listen to it
Or use onRepoChanged if it can be made to fire after remote operations (e.g., touch a file after push)
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: StatusBar.tsx and Sidebar.tsx both listen to onRepoChanged for refresh. After push/fetch/pull, if onRepoChanged doesn't fire, these components won't update either. The graph:force-refresh event should be listened to by all components that show git state.

userStories.title: GitHub OAuth Device Flow — Login with GitHub
userStories.description: As a user, I want to click Login with GitHub instead of manually creating and pasting a PAT.
acceptanceCriteria: GitHub OAuth App client_id is stored as a constant: Ov23liAVjXNTZR7qBroD
Login flow: POST https://github.com/login/device/code with client_id and scope=repo,read:user,read:org
Response includes device_code, user_code, verification_uri, interval
Show user a dialog: "Enter code XXXX-XXXX at github.com/login/device" with a button to open the URL
Poll POST https://github.com/login/oauth/access_token with client_id, device_code, grant_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code at the given interval
When user authorizes: response includes access_token — store it encrypted like PAT
Fetch user info with the token and add as an account (same as PAT flow)
Cancel button stops polling
Error handling: expired code, access denied, slow_down response
The Login with GitHub button is shown alongside the existing PAT input (user can choose either method)
All HTTP requests use https module in main process (not renderer) via IPC
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Client ID: Ov23liAVjXNTZR7qBroD. Device flow docs: https://docs.github.com/en/apps/oauth-apps/building-oauth-apps/authorizing-oauth-apps#device-flow. The polling endpoint returns error=authorization_pending while waiting, error=slow_down if polling too fast, or access_token on success. All API calls must go through main process (IPC) because renderer cant make cross-origin requests. Add IPC handlers: github:startDeviceFlow, github:pollDeviceFlow, github:cancelDeviceFlow.

userStories.title: GitLab Multi-Account Support
userStories.description: As a user, I want to connect multiple GitLab accounts (including self-hosted instances).
acceptanceCriteria: GitLab section in settings shows list of connected accounts (same pattern as GitHub multi-account)
Each account has: label, username, instance URL, avatar, remove button with confirmation
Add Account form: instance URL field (default gitlab.com), PAT input, label
Backend: gitlabAccounts array in electron-store, addAccount/getAccounts/removeAccount IPC handlers
Legacy single gitlabToken migrated to multi-account on first access
Each account stores its instance URL so different accounts can be on different GitLab instances
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Follow the exact same pattern as GitHub multi-account (US from mega-polish). The key difference: GitLab accounts have an instanceUrl field (defaults to https://gitlab.com). The API base is instanceUrl + /api/v4. GET /api/v4/user for user info.
